questions.prompt: What does 'eventually consistent' mean for KV?

choices.text: Writes propagate to all 300+ edge locations within ~60 seconds; reads return whatever's in the nearest edge cache (which may be stale during propagation). 'Just wrote, can I read it back?' works after a few seconds but not instantly. Optimized for reads — sub-millisecond from edge cache after first hit. Trade-off: scale and global distribution vs strong consistency. Right fit: sessions, feature flags, configuration, computed caches (where eventual consistency is fine). Wrong fit: transactional data, strict rate limits, anything requiring read-after-write guarantees. For strong consistency, use Durable Objects or D1 instead.

questions.explanation: Writes propagate within ~60s; reads serve from nearest cache (may be stale). Sub-ms reads.

questions.prompt: Need strict rate limiting (exactly 100 requests/min per user). KV or Durable Objects?

choices.text: Durable Objects. KV's eventual consistency means rate-limit counters can be undercounted (parallel requests in different edges see stale counts, race past the limit). For strict limits, use a Durable Object per user — it's a single coordinated instance globally, providing strong consistency. Each request increments the counter atomically; over-limit requests get rejected reliably. KV-based rate limiting works only for APPROXIMATE limits where some leakage is acceptable. For payment fraud prevention, abuse blocking, or any case where 'exactly 100' matters, Durable Objects.

questions.explanation: Durable Objects for strict consistency. KV is fine for approximate; not for exact-count limits.
